coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots(CQDs)-sensitized CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction as a photoelectrochemical biosensing platform for highly sensitive detection of prostate-specific antigen
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shunhua
Tang, Ximing
Zang, Linghe
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and quantitative detection of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) has been determined to be indispensable for clinical diagnostics of prostate cancer, whereas such detection is quite challenging due to the extremely low concentration of biomarkers in human serum samples. In this study, a photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor was effectively developed for the high-sensitivity analysis of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) using a signal amplification method utilizing sensitized carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In this experiment, cadmium sulfide quantum dots were employed as the substrate materials, and indium copper sulfide quantum dots were loaded on their surfaces. Moreover, the efficient matching of energy levels in these two materials contributed to the generation of photocurrents. The aforementioned heterojunction semiconductor QDs were thus combined with CQDs to produce CQDs on their surfaces. As a result of the presence of CQDs, the ability of heterojunction materials to absorb light was remarkably enhanced, increasing the photocurrent by over ten times. Consequently, in this study, CQDs were combined with PEC sensors, and the developed PEC biosensors exhibited excellent optical performance, sensitivity, repeatability, and stability. The results obtained from the analysis of actual samples were satisfactory and have promising application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical immunoassay
Carbon quantum dots
Prostate-specific antigen
CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electron transport mechanism of composite materials
Characterization of the fabricated PEC immunosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University
Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University ( YQ202206 ) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A)CuInS2; (B)CdS/CuInS2; (C)CdS/CuInS2/CQDs; (D) EDS image of the CdS/CuInS2/CQDs electrode; (E) HRTEM image of CdS/CuInS2 and (F)CdS/CuInS2/CQDs electrode.

(A) XRD patterns of CdS, CuInS2, CdS/CuInS2/CQDs and standard cards of CdS (PDF#97-003-1075) and CuInS2 (PDF#97-002-8739); (B) XPS spectra of the CdS/CuInS2/CQDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of (C) Cu 2p, (D) In 3d, (E) S 2p, (F) Cd 3d, (G) N 1s, (H) C 1s and (I) O 1s.
(A)UV–Vis diffuse-reflectance spectra of CdS, CuInS2, CdS/CuInS2 and CdS/CuInS2/CQDs; (B)The band gaps of CdS and (C) CuInS2. (D)Mott-Schottky plots of CdS and (E) CuInS2.
(A) Emission spectra of CQDs for varying excitation wavelengths; UPS spectra of the pure CdS (B) and CuInS2 (C); (D) Possible band bending mechanism of the PEC sensor.

(A) Photocurrent responses of CuInS2, CdS and CdS/CuInS2; (B) Stability of the CdS/CuInS2/CQDs electrode; (C) EIS Nyquist plots: (a) FTO; (b) CdS/FTO; (C) CdS/CuInS2/FTO; (d) CdS/CuInS2/CQDs/FTO; (e) CdS/CuInS2/CQDs/Ab/FTO; (f) CdS/CuInS2/CQDs/Ab/BSA/FTO; (g) CdS/CuInS2/CQDs/Ab/BSA/PSA/FTO; (D) Photocurrent responses: (a) CuInS2; (b) CdS/CuInS2; (c) CdS/CuInS2/CQDs; (d) CdS/CuInS2/CQDs/Ab; (e) CdS/CuInS2/CQDs/Ab/BSA; (f) CdS/CuInS2/CQDs/Ab/BSA/PSA.

(A) Photocurrent response and (B) Linear curve of the sensor used for PSA detection; (C) Specificity detection: (a) AFP, (b) CEA, (c) cyfra21-1, (d) ALP, (e) HSA, (f) PSA, (g) AFP + PSA, (h) CEA + PSA, (i) cyfra21-1 + PSA, (j) ALP + PSA, (k) HSA + PSA; (CPSA = 100 ng/mL, CX = 10 ng/mL) (D) Stability of the sensor (CPSA = 100 ng/mL). (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots(CQDs)-sensitized CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction as a photoelectrochemical biosensing platform for highly sensitive detection of prostate-specific antigen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science and Bio-pharmaceuticals, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science and Bio-pharmaceuticals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life science and Bio-pharmaceuticals, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, China.
Corresponding author. School of Life science and Bio-pharmaceuticals, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life science and Bio-pharmaceuticals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and quantitative detection of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) has been determined to be indispensable for clinical diagnostics of prostate cancer, whereas such detection is quite challenging due to the extremely low concentration of biomarkers in human serum samples. In this study, a photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor was effectively developed for the high-sensitivity analysis of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) using a signal amplification method utilizing sensitized carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In this experiment, cadmium sulfide quantum dots were employed as the substrate materials, and indium copper sulfide quantum dots were loaded on their surfaces. Moreover, the efficient matching of energy levels in these two materials contributed to the generation of photocurrents. The aforementioned heterojunction semiconductor QDs were thus combined with CQDs to produce CQDs on their surfaces. As a result of the presence of CQDs, the ability of heterojunction materials to absorb light was remarkably enhanced, increasing the photocurrent by over ten times. Consequently, in this study, CQDs were combined with PEC sensors, and the developed PEC biosensors exhibited excellent optical performance, sensitivity, repeatability, and stability. The results obtained from the analysis of actual samples were satisfactory and have promising application prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate cancer is the most prevalent malignancy among men [1]. In previous studies, prostate-specific antigen (PSA) was determined to be a key indicator to diagnose prostate cancers [2]. On the condition that serum levels exceed 4 ng/mL, it may lead to prostate cancer [3–5]. Numerous technologies have been developed thus far to detect PSA, including electrochemiluminescence [6,7], electrochemistry [8], surface plasmon resonance [9], as well as fluorescence spectrophotometry [10]. Nevertheless, additional constraints remain, including restricted sensitivity and operational intricacy. For early clinical diagnosis and therapy monitoring, therefore, the development of a highly selective and sensitive analytical method to detect PSA is of the utmost importance.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) immunosensor, as a promising bioanalytical technique, has garnered extensive admiration [11,12]. This technology combines the specificity and affinity of antibody-antigen reactions with the high sensitivity of electrochemical methods and has tremendous potential for diagnostics applications. Furthermore, the selection of working electrodes for photoelectrochemical sensors is the key to realizing the sensitive detection of photoelectrochemistry, nonetheless, how to further enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of photoelectrochemical sensors remains a challenging task. For the purpose of addressing the above problems, modification methods including elemental doping, precious metal surface deposition, and heterojunction construction have been proposed to enhance the sensing performance [12,13]. A considerable proportion of these researchers are dedicated to the development of heterojunctions as a means to fabricate and design optoelectronic materials that exhibit exceptional optoelectronic characteristics. By rationally designing and constructing heterojunctions, the selectivity and sensitivity of photoelectrochemical sensors can be enhanced, expanding their potential for applications in biomedical analysis and clinical research [14–16]. For instance, hollow SnO2/CdS QDs/CdCO3 heterostructured nanocubes were synthesized for the detection of cytokeratin 19 fragment 21–1 with satisfactory results in the clinical diagnosis of non-small cell lung cancer. The charge transfer distance is shortened and a significant number of photoactive sites are provided by the hollow structure; this significantly enhances energy conversion and photon absorption efficiency [17]. Moreover, Zhu et al. further developed a PEC aptasensor exhibiting excellent selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of human telomerase activity, on the basis of the Ag2S/Ag/ZnIn2S4/C3N4 heterostructure. Furthermore, the constructed multi-component/multi-phase heterostructures with high photo activity effectively improved charge separation and transfer between layers, essentially augmented the electron migration rate, and reduced the possibility of photogenerated electron-hole complexation, thus enhancing the response speed and sensitivity of the sensor [18]. Additionally, there was also a constructed PEC sensor based on CuS/ZnIn2S4 flower-like heterostructures that demonstrated great potential for the assay of vascular endothelial growth factor 165. The active sites were rendered more accessible and the specific surface area was increased in the synthesized heterojunction structure, as a result, the catalytic activity and photoelectric conversion efficiency were ultimately enhanced [19].

In recent years, due to their unique optical and electronic characteristics, quantum dots (QDs) have become a focal point of investigation in the field of PEC chemical and biological detection and sensing platforms [20,21]. Among them, CdS QDs have attracted much attention due to their narrow forbidden bandwidth, and yet the severe electron-hole pair complexation and accompanying photo corrosion of single CdS QDs greatly limit their application in PEC [22]. Consequently, compositing CdS quantum dots with other materials to form heterojunctions contributes to photo-induced charge separation and enhances the PEC photocurrent response. Furthermore, CuInS2 quantum dots composed of group I-III-VI elements are promising semiconductors that are green, low-toxic, and environmentally friendly, as well as have a suitable bandgap (Eg = 1.5 eV), excellent light absorption coefficients (α > 105 cm−1), and excellent photostability in the visible spectrum [23]. Moreover, the photoelectric conversion efficiency can be substantially enhanced due to the energy band positions of CdS and CuInS2 being matched to promote the effective separation and transport of photogenerated electrons and holes. Meanwhile, the integration of both components has the potential to increase the sensor's light absorption range while also enhancing its photostability and chemical stability, which makes the CdS/CuInS2 composite material promises to be an ideal material combination for constructing highly efficient, stable, and tunable photoelectrochemical sensor platforms.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new nano-carbon material, have received researchers' interest due to numerous advantages, including low toxicity, low cost, remarkable biocompatibility, and luminescence emission characteristics in relation to size and wavelength [24–26]. Since CQDs have amorphous nanocrystalline cores consisting predominantly of graphite or turbo-layered carbon (sp2 carbon), or graphene or graphene oxide fused with diamond-like sp3 hybridized carbon inserts, CQDs develop the capacity for electron storage and transfer. Due to this unique property, CQDs have been developed in the domain of PEC to enhance the light availability of photoactive materials including CQDs/TiO2 [27], CQDs/C3N4 [28], CQDs/BiOBr [29], etc.

As the experiment itself documented, PSA in real samples was quantitatively detected using a PEC sensor on the basis of CQDs sensitized signal amplification strategy. Moreover, CdS QDs were employed as the substrate material and CuInS2 QDs were loaded on its surface to generate photocurrent owing to the efficient matching of energy levels of the two materials. In light of this, CQDs were synthesized on the surface of the heterojunction. Furthermore, CQDs with upconversion luminescence effect can favorably absorb long-wavelength visible and near-infrared light and upconvert it to short-wavelength UV light, which additionally stimulates the heterojunction material to produce additional electrons and holes [30,31]. The PEC sensor was integrated with CQDs to create a PEC platform with exceptional optical properties, including sensitivity, repeatability, and stability. Real samples were utilized to demonstrate the sensitivity of PSA detection. Hence, the proposed sensor paws the way for developing a novel approach to the measurement of biomarkers.
Reagents and instruments are detailed in Supporting Information.

The synthesis of CuInS2 and CdS/CuInS2 composites were given in Supporting Information. According to the literature, CQDs were further attached to the CdS/CuInS2 composites [32]. Briefly, 0.05 g of prepared CdS/CuInS2 composites, 0.0541 g of o-phenylenediamine, and 0.0739 g of d-glutamic acid were added to 30 mL of deionized water in a tube. The mixed solution was then loaded into a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) reactor at 180 °C for 6 h. Afterward, solid products were washed and dried to acquire CdS/CuInS2/CQDs composites.

Prior to PEC sensor preparation, the FTO electrodes (1 × 2.0 cm2) were sequentially sonicated with acetone, anhydrous ethanol and deionized water for 30 min. The preparation of ternary heterojunction CdS/CuInS2/CQDs PEC immunosensors is demonstrated in Scheme 1 . Firstly, CdS/CuInS2/CQDs suspension (3 mg/mL) was uniformly introduced guttatim to the electrodes, and dried at 120 °C for 6 h. Afterward, 100 μL of PSA antibody (10 μg/mL) was fixed onto the surface of the CdS/CuInS2/CQDs electrodes. Next, 100 μL 1 wt% bovineserum albumin (BSA) was operated the modified electrodes to eliminate the non-specific binding sites. Subsequently, 100 μL of PSA antigen was uniformly dropped onto the electrodes and incubated for 90 min to produce specific binding. To remove excess free protein, the modified electrodes were rinsed with PBS buffer (0.01 mol/L) after each modification step.

The morphological information of CuInS2, CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction and CdS/CuInS2/CQDs composites acquired through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) are depicted in Fig. 1 . Here, the CuInS2 QDs were spherical with a uniform structure (50 nm), as described in Fig. 1A. Fig. 1B indicates that the CuInS2 QDs are uniformly dispersed across the surface of CdS, suggesting effective synthesis of CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction. Afterward, the elemental composition of the CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction was examined by utilizing energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). Moreover, the EDS image and elemental mapping of heterojunction revealed that the material consists of Cd, S, Cu, and In elements, as depicted in Fig. S1. From the analysis of Fig. 1C, the material's outer surface exhibits a relatively rough texture and is adorned with a significant number of CQDs particles, which proved that CQDs had been accurately loaded onto the surface of the CdS/CuInS2 composite. Afterward, the elemental composition of the composites were tested with the help of energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The presence of Cu, In, S, Cd, O, and N in the composites is depicted in Fig. 1D and Fig. S2 through elemental mapping. Fig. 1E–and F demonstrated the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the materials, respectively. The size distribution lattice spacing of the material was obtained from the HRTEM images. 0.343 nm and 0.334 nm can be attributed to the (111) and (002) planes of CdS [33]. The lattice stripes of 0.321 and 0.196 nm for CuInS2 can be attributed to the (112) and (204) planes of CuInS2, respectively [34]. On the contrary, the lattice stripe of CQDs is 0.236 nm, which is associated with the graphite lattice of CQDs [35]. This finding aligns with the outcomes derived from SEM images.

Employing an X-ray diffractometer (XRD), the crystal structures of the fabricated substances were examined. Besides, the XRD images of CuInS2, CdS and CdS/CuInS2/CQDs are displayed in Fig. 2 A, which are primarily the same as those previously reported [36,37]. The XRD pattern of CdS corresponds to the standard card (PDF#97-003-1075). Moreover, the characteristic peak of CdS at 2θ at 26.5° is associated with the (111) plane. The XRD pattern of CuInS2 can be credited effectively with the standard card (PDF#97-002-8739). The peaks of CuInS2 at 2θ of 27.9° and 46.7° of the (112) and (204) planes, respectively, are evidently illustrated in the figure. There is a clear correspondence between the prepared CdS/CuInS2/CQDs composites and the standard card. Additionally, the elemental composition and valence states in CdS/CuInS2/CQDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2B illustrates that the surface of the CdS/CuInS2/CQDs heterojunction is covered with the elements Cu, In, S, Cd, N, and O. A signal peak with an energy of 930.86 eV, corresponding to Cu 2p3/2 [38], can be observed in the Cu 2p plot of Fig. 2C. Two peaks appear at 444.97 eV and 452.53 eV, in relation to In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2 in Fig. 2D, in agreement with the reported literature [39]. The S 2p spectrum exhibited double peaks at 161.40 and 162.56 eV (Fig. 2E), for 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively [40]. The XPS Cd 3d peaks at 405.01 and 411.72 eV (Fig. 2F) were indexed to the classical Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively [41]. In Fig. 2G, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s indicates a characteristic peak at 404.97 eV, suggesting the appearance of NH2 groups in the material. The peak at 284.6 eV that has been observed is indicative of carbon that is intrinsic to the instrument [42]. Moreover, the peak at 288.7 eV reveals the appearance of element C in the form of carbonyl CO in the material (Fig. 2H). The XPS O 1s peaks at 531.54 and 533.15 eV implied the appearance of C–O and CO groups in the material (Fig. 2I) [35]. The XPS patterns unequivocally indicated the simultaneous presence of CdS, CuInS2, and CQDs, which is consistent with the outcomes that have been previously identified.

The optical absorption properties of the synthesized materials were determined using UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS). As demonstrated in Fig. 3 A, the optical absorption of CuInS2 is extremely weak in the visible range, and the absorption of CdS/CuInS2 and CdS/CuInS2/CQDs is substantially stronger than that of CuInS2. In comparison with CuInS2, the heterojunction material composed of CdS and CuInS2 remarkably enhances the optical absorption at the absorption edge 250–550 nm. Furthermore, the optical absorption capability within the near-infrared range of 550–800 nm was significantly enhanced upon the combination of CQDs and the CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction. This suggests that the utilization of CQDs as photosensitizers for the CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction yielded satisfactory results.

The band gap energy can be obtained by determining the correlation between (αhν)2 and optical energy (hν), which is based on the Kubelka-Munk theory, to determine the correlation between the band gap Eg and the absorption coefficient α [43]. The equation is (αhν) 2 ∝hν – E
g . On the basis of the Tauc plot method, the band gap energies of CdS and CuInS2 were calculated to be 2.32 and 1.69 eV, respectively (Fig. 3B and C). Mott-Schottky curves were utilized to investigate the flat-band potentials of CdS and CuInS2, thereby providing additional insight into the electron transfer mechanism of CdS/CuInS2. Tangent to the longest straight-line portion of the Mott-Schottky curve is described in Fig. 3D and E.The point where the tangent line intersects with the X-axis represents a flat-band potential. The flat-band potential (0.2 eV) for n-type semiconductors is higher by comparison with the conduction band (CB). Consequently, the values of the CB of CdS and CuInS2 relative to the standard hydrogen electrode can be measured as −0.255 and −0.442 eV, respectively. Additionally, on the basis of the bandgap width Eg measured by UV diffuse reflectance and the equation Eg = EVB-ECB [44], the relative valence band (VB) potentials of CdS and CuInS2 for the standard hydrogen electrode are 2.065 eV and 1.248 eV in accordance with calculations, respectively. Overall, their findings align with those documented in the literature [45,46].

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the CQDs solution can emit shorter wavelengths of light at longer wavelengths of excitation light, exhibiting an obvious upconversion luminescence effect. When the excitation wavelength rises, the intensity of emission peak of CQDs solution firstly increases and then decreases with obvious excitation dependence. At an excitation wavelength (＜650 nm), the solution of CQDs is capable of emitting short-wavelength light below 400 nm that can be utilized to excite CuInS2 to generate additional electron-hole pairs. Consequently, it is advantageous to modify composites using CQDs to improve the effectiveness of light absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron transport mechanism of composite materials
ce:section.ce:para: The enhancement of photogenerated carrier separation occurs due to the existence of an intrinsic electric domain at the interface of the composite material. This domain arises from the variation in fermi energy levels. The fermi energy level reached the highest energy level occupied by an electron at T = 0K. The work function (Φ) is the lowest energy required to move an electron from the interior of a solid to the surface of an object [47]. The value of the work function is equal to the variation between the vacuum level (Ev) and the fermi level (EF). The equation is: Φ = Ev - EF. In order to identify the energy band positions of CdS and CuInS2, the value of the work function may be ascertained based on the ultraviolet photo-electron spectroscopy (UPS) with the equation of Φ = hv + Ecut off - EF. As demonstrated in Fig. 4B and C, the UPS diagrams for both CdS and CuInS2 materials are illustrated in the figure. The light source used is He Ⅰ (21.22 eV), EF denotes the differentiation of the fermi edge, and Ecut off is the intersection of the straight line fitted by the cut-off edge with the baseline. As shown in the figure, the work functions (Ф) of CdS and CuInS2 are 4.87 and 5.39 eV, respectively, while the variation between the vacuum energy level and the potential of the standard hydrogen electrode is 4.5 eV. Hence, the fermi energy levels of CdS and CuInS2 in terms of the standard hydrogen electrode are 0.37 and 0.89 eV, respectively.

On the condition that the two ends of the heterojunction are in contact, the carriers undergo net migration due to the obvious staggered arrangement structure of the energy bands of CdS and CuInS2. And the fermi energy levels at the two ends are not the same, contributing to energy band bending. As displayed in Fig. 4D, the energy levels of CdS and CuInS2 will be respectively shifted downward and upward, and ultimately attain fermi energy level equilibrium [48]. Meanwhile, a built-in electric field arose at the P–N interface of CdS and CuInS2. The electron transport path of the PEC sensor is depicted in Scheme 2 . At the CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction, both CdS and CuInS2 are excited to produce reactive holes and photogenerated electrons when exposed to incident light. Afterward, photogenerated electrons are switched from the CB of CuInS2 to CdS and flow to the FTO electrode owing to the matching energy band structure in the CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction and the built-in electric field. Likewise, the holes flow from the VB of CdS to CuInS2 and are finally removed by the electron donor AA, accomplishing the transfer and separation of holes and electrons. Subsequently, the enhanced photocurrent mechanism of CQDs in composites was investigated in greater depth. When the composite was irradiated by light, the electrons on the CB of CuInS2 were easily transformed to CQDs, thus inhibiting charge recombination [49]. Secondly, the CQDs loaded on the heterojunction are electron reservoirs to acquire electrons in CuInS2, which further enhances the charge separation [50]. Finally, CQDs with the upconversion luminescence effect is able to absorb long-wavelength visible and near-infrared light and upconvert it to short-wavelength UV light to further excite the heterojunction material to generate more electrons and holes [51]. The specific reaction equations are illuminated as follows:
CdS/CuInS2/CQDs + hν → h+ +e-
h+ +AA → dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA)

The CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction is a typical semiconductor heterostructure in which CdS is an n-type semiconductor and CuInS2 acts as a p-type semiconductor. The energy bands of the semiconductors will be deflected and the Fermi energy levels will be in equilibrium when p-type and n-type semiconductors come into contact. This is due to the formation of a space charge region caused by the diffusion of electrons and holes [23,52]. Consequently, the built-in potential in the space charge region guides the electrons and holes to move rapidly in opposite directions, contributing to an enhanced level of separation of the electron-hole pairs, and both can be excited concurrently to generate electron-hole pairs of their respective elements [53]. Due to the energy level matching, electrons are transported from CuInS2 to the conduction band of CdS. Simultaneously, holes in CdS are transported to the valence band of CuInS2. The photocurrent is produced through the recombination of electrons and holes in the heterojunction. In the CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction, the key to the electron transfer mechanism is energy level matching and energy band bending. Energy level matching enhances the segregation and accumulation of electrons and holes, thereby augmenting the production of photocurrents, whereas energy level matching guarantees the efficient transport of electrons and holes between the two substances [54].

In CdS/CuInS2/CQDs composites, CQDs play the role of light absorption and electron-hole pair generation. On the condition that light is irradiated into the CQDs/CuInS2/CdS composites, photons are absorbed by the CQDs layer and electron-hole pairs are generated. Further transport and separation of these photogenerated electrons and holes is a critical procedure in heterojunctions as well as affects the photovoltaic conversion performance [55]. In the CQDs layer, photogenerated electrons and holes are bound in small quantum dots. Owing to the quantum confinement effect, the energy band structure of the CQDs layer exhibits discrete energy levels. The existence of these distinct energy levels confers extended lifetimes on photogenerated electrons and holes, thereby facilitating additional transport [56]. When photogenerated electrons are transported from the CQDs layer to the CuInS2 layer, the matching of the energy band structure plays a crucial role. If the energy band structure can be suitably paired, the electrons can be smoothly transmitted under the continuous distribution of the energy bands, minimizing reflection and scattering losses. When the photogenerated electrons reach the CuInS2 layer, they continue to travel down to the CdS layer. In the CdS layer, the photogenerated electrons continue to be transferred and participate in the photoelectrochemical reaction. Moreover, the photogenerated electrons allow for additional transfer and be utilized in the CdS layer due to their better electron transfer capability and photoelectrochemical reaction activity, and the role of ascorbic acid in it is reflected in the following aspects, firstly, under the light, ascorbic acid can be excited and release electrons [57]. These released electrons can further participate in the electron transport in CdS/CuInS2/CQDs composites. The holes in the CQDs can be filled by the electrons liberated by ascorbic acid, thereby accelerating the generation of electron-hole pairs, which ultimately enhances the photocurrent generation in the heterojunction. Additionally, ascorbic acid can form an electron transport pathway at the interface with CQDs, CdS, and CuInS2, which contributes to electron transport between these materials. By introducing this supplementary electron transport pathway, the photocurrent output can be increased even further while the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers is enhanced [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the fabricated PEC immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The layer-by-layer photocurrent response of the synthesized materials is depicted in Fig. 5 A. In comparison with CdS and CuInS2, the photocurrent intensity can be successfully increased by the CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction. The increased photocurrent intensity results in the stepped energy band structure between CdS and CuInS2, which promotes the separation and shift of electrons and holes. Besides, as is evident in Fig. 5B, the prepared CdS/CuInS2/CQDs electrodes were tested for photocurrent stability. There were no substantial alterations observed in the photocurrent of the modified electrode following ten switching cycles. And it was found that the photocurrent signal of the synthesized CdS/CuInS2/CQDs electrode is stable and can be employed to design an excellent PEC sensor.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the CdS/CuInS2/CQDs improved electrodes is shown in Fig. 5C. The linear part denotes the diffusion-limited process with lower frequency. The diameter of the semicircle, which possesses a higher frequency, denotes the electron transfer resistance (Ret), while the semicircle itself denotes an electron transfer-limited process. As demonstrated in Fig. 5C, the bare FTO electrode exhibited a smaller Ret (curve a), whereas the addition of the CdS caused a slight rise in the Ret value (curve b). Afterward, the Ret value slightly increased due to the attachment of CdS/CuInS2 heterojunction (curve c). Following coating the FTO surface with CdS/CuInS2/CQDs heterojunctions, Ret further increased (curve d). The Ret of the electrodes exhibited a gradual increase as Ab, BSA, and PSA were added to the PEC sensor (curves e-g). The stems from the large spatial resistivity effect of protein molecules and the insulating effect that impedes the diffusion of redox probes to the electrode surface, demonstrating that the PEC sensor was effectively modified. As can be noticed in Fig. 5D, similarly, this experiment measured the photocurrent intensity of the different electrodes at each stage. The photocurrent intensity exhibited a progressive decline as Ab, BSA, and PSA were added, which corresponded to the outcomes of EIS. This confirmed the successful preparation of the designed PEC biosensor.

The ability of the PEC platform to detect PSA was evaluated under the optimum immunoassay conditions, which are detailed in Supporting Information (Fig. S3). The photocurrent response related to PSA concentration is depicted in Fig. 6 A, where the photocurrent of the biosensing platform reduced when increasing PSA concentration. The calibration curve of the PEC platform is shown in Fig. 6B. The variation of photocurrent was well linearized with the logarithm of PSA concentration between 0.001 and 10000 ng/mL, fitting a linear regression equation of I = −6.556lgCPSA (ng/mL) +130.3 (R2 = 0.9957). In addition, the LOD of AFP was calculated was calculated as 0.07 pg/mL according to equation 3σ/S. In comparison with other reported sensors (Table S1), the PEC biosensor constructed in this experiment had a larger linear range while lower limit, indicating the excellent property of the designed PEC platform.

To examine the selectivity of the PEC sensor, the photocurrent of the sensor was measured in the existence of other interfering substances, including alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), cyfra21-1, alkaline phosphatase (ALP), and human serum albumin (HSA). Notably, replacing PSA with other interfering substances incubated on the sensor did not result in significant photocurrent change values (Fig. 6C). Nonetheless, in the presence of PSA, the photocurrent change values were quite steady after operating high concentrations of interfering substances on the electrode. It was demonstrated that this PEC sensor is highly selective to PSA detection.

The development and application of the sensor might be affected by the stability of the sensor. Therefore, it is a critical parameter of PEC sensors. As depicted in Fig. 6D, examining the sensor for ten switching cycles revealed no significant variations in photocurrent, thereby confirming its satisfactory stability. Furthermore, the PEC biosensor was stored at 4 °C and removed for detection after 7 d. No obvious decrease appeared in the photocurrent intensity. Numerous outcomes demonstrated that the PEC sensor that was fabricated exhibited exceptional stability. Meanwhile, the PSA solution with a concentration of 100 ng/mL was measured in parallel with 5 electrodes synthesized under identical conditions (Fig. S4). The relative standard deviation (RSD) value was 4.1%, indicating the excellent reproducibility of the fabricated PEC sensor.

Aiming to examine the practicality of the method, actual samples of healthy adult serum were used. PSA concentrations in the serum of healthy adults are typically below 4 ng/mL. Experiments were performed by adding different concentrations of PSA to the real samples using the standard addition approach and the findings were determined. As can be seen in Table S2, the RSD values were below 2.5% and the findings ranged from 92.1% to 106.8%. The findings demonstrated that the established biosensor platform is precise and suitable for the quantification of PSA concentrations in actual samples.

A PEC immunosensor for PSA detection was prepared and validated. As a consequence of the matched energy band structure between CdS and CuInS2 and the built-in electric field, the separation and transfer of electrons and holes were accelerated. Given this foundation, CQDs with an upconversion luminescence effect can profitably absorb long-wavelength visible light and near-infrared light and upconvert it to short-wavelength ultraviolet light to further excite the heterojunction material to generate more electrons and holes. In addition, CQDs can be used as electron reservoirs to further improve charge separation. Except for that, the constructed PEC sensor demonstrated satisfactory stability, high sensitivity and anti-interference ability, and had been effectively adopted for the quantitative detection of PSA in real samples. The practical PEC sensing platform provides certain new ideas and strategies for other disease biomarkers detection.

Shunhua Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ximing Tang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software. Linghe Zang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Longshan Zhao: Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (YQ202206) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.125811.
